extra.message:
Missing authentication on POST / (index) route (state-changing handler exposed)

The router exposes a POST handler for '/' without any route-level or visible handler-level authentication enforcement in this chunk. Per checklist, each POST handler must verify authentication server-side; otherwise unauthenticated users can perform state-changing actions.

Impact: Unauthenticated attackers may be able to create/modify server state or trigger privileged actions implemented in views.index (unknown here), potentially leading to data tampering or abuse.

Fix: In views.index POST branch, require authentication (session user check / decorator) and enforce authorization for any affected resources. Add rate limiting for form submissions.

Potential missing authentication/authorization on state-changing routes (IDOR/data exposure risk)

Multiple routes accept POST requests and access resources by numeric IDs in the URL (students/{id}, courses/{id}, evaluate/{student_id}/{course_id}, review). There is no indication at the routing layer that authentication/authorization is required, raising risk that handlers allow unauthenticated access or insecure direct object reference (IDOR) to other users' data.

Impact: If handlers do not enforce authz, attackers can create/modify/read other users’ records by iterating IDs, leading to PII exposure and unauthorized changes.

Fix: Enforce authentication and per-object authorization in each handler (views.*). Use ownership checks and avoid exposing sequential IDs where possible (or add access controls). Add tests to ensure unauthorized requests are rejected.

Authentication bypass via UI-only checks: templates hide admin forms but backend endpoints are unprotected

The template conditionally shows the 'Add new course' form only for admin users, but the corresponding backend POST handler in views.py does not enforce admin authorization. This is a common anti-pattern where authorization relies on URL/UI structure rather than server-side checks.

Impact: Attackers can bypass UI restrictions by directly POSTing to endpoints, leading to unauthorized data creation and stored XSS injection.

Fix: Enforce authorization in the handler (server-side) for all state-changing actions; treat templates as presentation only.

Potential IDOR risk: numeric IDs in routes without visible per-handler authorization enforcement

Routes expose direct object identifiers (students/{id}, courses/{id}, evaluate/{student_id}/... , courses/{course_id}/review). Without explicit authorization checks in the corresponding handlers, users may access/modify other users’ objects by changing IDs. This is flagged here because route design suggests object-level access control is needed; confirm in handlers.

Impact: Unauthorized read/write of other users’ data (PII, grades/reviews) by iterating IDs.

Fix: In each handler, enforce authentication and object-level authorization (verify the current user is allowed to access/modify the referenced student/course). Use non-guessable IDs where appropriate and add access control checks server-side.

CSRF protection middleware implemented but disabled in application middleware chain

The CSRF middleware is commented out, so POST requests are not protected by CSRF token validation. Multiple state-changing routes exist (e.g., create/update actions) and will be vulnerable to cross-site request forgery.

Impact: An attacker can trick an authenticated victim’s browser into submitting forged POST requests, causing unauthorized actions (e.g., creating/modifying records, submitting reviews/evaluations, logging out).

Fix: Enable CSRF middleware globally (uncomment and import it) and ensure all state-changing endpoints validate CSRF tokens. Consider SameSite cookies as defense-in-depth.

Jinja2 autoescape disabled globally enabling stored/reflected XSS across templates

Jinja2 is configured with autoescape=False, disabling HTML escaping by default. Any template rendering user-controlled content becomes an XSS sink unless every variable is manually escaped.

Impact: Attackers can inject JavaScript via any user-controlled field rendered in templates (e.g., names, titles, reviews), leading to session theft, CSRF bypass, account takeover, and data exfiltration.

Fix: Enable autoescaping (autoescape=True or select_autoescape for HTML/XML). Audit templates for unsafe use of |safe and ensure proper escaping/encoding for the output context.

SQL injection in Student.create() via Python string interpolation into INSERT query

Student.create() builds the SQL INSERT statement by interpolating the untrusted `name` value directly into the query string using Python `%` formatting. If `name` contains quotes or SQL metacharacters, an attacker can alter the query (e.g., terminate the string and inject additional SQL).

Impact: Arbitrary SQL execution in the database context (data exfiltration/modification, authentication/authorization bypass depending on DB permissions).

Fix: Use parameterized queries: `q = 'INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%s)'` and `await cur.execute(q, (name,))`. Avoid manual quoting and string formatting for SQL.

CSRF middleware only checks POST and is not applied; state-changing endpoints remain unprotected

Even though a csrf_middleware exists, it is not enabled in the app middleware stack. Additionally, it only checks request.method == 'POST', leaving other state-changing methods (PUT/PATCH/DELETE) unprotected if added later.

Impact: CSRF remains exploitable across the application; future endpoints using other HTTP verbs would also be vulnerable.

Fix: Enable the middleware and validate CSRF for all unsafe methods (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE). Consider double-submit or per-request tokens; avoid popping token if you want multi-tab usability.

Authentication bypass via UI-only checks: students creation form shown only to auth_user but backend allows unauthenticated POST

The template shows the 'Add new student' form only when auth_user is present, but the backend /students POST handler does not require authentication. Attackers can bypass by direct POST.

Impact: Unauthorized student creation and stored XSS injection via student names.

Fix: Add authentication/authorization checks in views.students POST branch; do not rely on template conditions.

Missing authorization: evaluate endpoint allows any user (or unauthenticated) to create marks

The evaluate handler performs a state-changing action (creating marks) but has no `@authorize` guard and no admin check. Although the UI shows it only for admins, an attacker can POST directly to the endpoint to create/alter marks.

Impact: Unauthorized grade manipulation; integrity compromise; potential stored XSS via error rendering if validation errors are attacker-controlled.

Fix: Protect with `@authorize(ensure_admin=True)` and server-side authorization checks; enforce CSRF.

Missing authentication/authorization on Students POST allows unauthenticated student creation

The /students/ handler processes POST requests and calls Student.create() without any authentication guard (e.g., @authorize) or explicit permission check. Any unauthenticated user can create student records.

Impact: Unauthenticated data creation; potential database pollution, stored XSS payload insertion via student name, and integrity compromise of PII records.

Fix: Require authentication and appropriate authorization (e.g., admin-only) for POST. Add @authorize() or explicit checks (auth_user and role) before calling Student.create(). Enforce server-side validation and CSRF protection.

Courses creation endpoint missing authentication (unauthenticated POST can create courses)

The /courses/ handler processes POST requests to create a course without any server-side authentication check. The template shows the form only to admins, but attackers can POST directly.

Impact: Unauthenticated attackers can create arbitrary courses (data integrity compromise; potential stored XSS via title/description).

Fix: Require authentication in the handler (e.g., @authorize()) and enforce admin-only authorization for course creation.

Potential SQL injection / unsafe query construction in Course.get_many() LIMIT/OFFSET handling

Course.get_many() dynamically concatenates LIMIT/OFFSET clauses into the SQL string. While it attempts to use placeholders, the query text is still assembled conditionally and uses a nonstandard pattern (`LIMIT + %(limit)s`, `OFFSET + %(offset)s`) and passes parameters via `**params`, which can lead to driver-specific formatting behavior and increases risk of SQL injection if the driver performs string interpolation or if future edits concatenate raw values. LIMIT/OFFSET are common injection points when not strictly parameterized/validated as integers.

Impact: If exploitable, attacker could manipulate pagination parameters to alter SQL execution, potentially extracting or modifying data.

Fix: Avoid concatenating SQL fragments with user-controlled pagination. Validate `limit`/`offset` as bounded integers and use the driver’s supported parameter style consistently (e.g., `... LIMIT %s OFFSET %s` with a tuple). Consider hard caps and default limits.

Weak password hashing using unsalted MD5 in User.check_password()

Passwords are verified by comparing against an MD5 hash of the provided password. MD5 is fast and broken for password storage; without a per-user salt and a slow KDF, hashes are vulnerable to offline cracking and rainbow tables.

Impact: If the users table is leaked (via SQLi or other compromise), attackers can crack passwords quickly and reuse them across services; enables account takeover.

Fix: Use a modern password hashing algorithm (Argon2id, bcrypt, scrypt, or PBKDF2 with strong parameters) with per-user salts. Store algorithm parameters with the hash and perform constant-time comparisons.

Session cookie HttpOnly disabled in RedisStorage allowing client-side script access

RedisStorage is configured with httponly=False, which makes the session cookie accessible to JavaScript. Combined with XSS risk, this enables straightforward session theft.

Impact: If any XSS occurs, attackers can read and exfiltrate session cookies, hijacking user sessions and accessing PII.

Fix: Set httponly=True. Also set Secure=True (HTTPS only) and SameSite=Lax/Strict where supported. Consider rotating session IDs on login and privilege changes.

Potential information disclosure: rendering exception objects into error templates with autoescape disabled

Error handlers pass the raw exception object into templates. With global autoescape disabled, any exception message containing user-controlled content could lead to reflected XSS; additionally, templates may expose stack/context details depending on how they render `error`.

Impact: Reflected XSS via error messages and/or leakage of internal error details that aid exploitation.

Fix: Do not render raw exception objects; pass only safe, generic messages and log details server-side. Re-enable autoescape. Ensure templates escape error strings and avoid printing stack traces in production.

Reflected/stored XSS risk: unescaped auth_user name fields rendered in base template (autoescape disabled context)

User profile fields (first_name/middle_name/last_name) are rendered without explicit escaping. With Jinja2 autoescape disabled (per app context), these values can inject HTML/JS.

Impact: Stored XSS affecting any page using base.jinja2 when an attacker can set their name fields; session theft, CSRF, account takeover.

Fix: Enable Jinja2 autoescaping globally and/or apply the |e filter to all user-controlled fields. Consider output encoding in templates by default.

Stored XSS risk: course title rendered unescaped in course page title block

course.name is rendered in the <title> block without escaping. If autoescape is disabled, attacker-controlled course fields can inject markup into the page head.

Impact: Stored XSS via course fields; can execute in victims' browsers when viewing course pages.

Fix: Use |e for all course fields and enable autoescape globally. Also ensure consistent field naming (title vs name) to avoid unexpected values.

Stored XSS risk: course title and description rendered unescaped in courses list

course.title and course.description are rendered without escaping in the course list. If autoescape is disabled, attacker-controlled content can inject HTML/JS.

Impact: Stored XSS on the courses listing page; broad exposure to all visitors.

Fix: Apply |e to course.title/description and enable autoescape globally.

Information disclosure: internal exception attributes exposed in 50x error template

The 50x error page renders error.__dict__ to users, which can include stack/context details, configuration, file paths, SQL queries, and other sensitive internals.

Impact: Sensitive information disclosure aiding exploitation (e.g., SQLi, path discovery, secrets in exception context).

Fix: Remove internal error details from user-facing templates. Log details server-side and show a generic error message with a correlation ID.

Stored XSS risk: course.title and course.description rendered unescaped in student results

course.title and course.description are rendered without escaping in the student page. If course fields are attacker-controlled, this is a stored XSS sink when autoescape is disabled.

Impact: Stored XSS on student pages; broad exposure.

Fix: Apply |e to course.title/description and enable autoescape.

Stored XSS risk: student names rendered unescaped in students list

Student names are rendered without escaping in the students list. Since student creation is unprotected, an attacker can store XSS payloads in names and have them execute for visitors.

Impact: Stored XSS on students listing page; can target authenticated users/admins.

Fix: Escape name with |e and enable autoescape. Also protect student creation endpoint.

Sensitive object injected into template context (auth_user) may expose PII/secrets

The auth_user_processor injects the full auth_user object into every template context. If this object contains sensitive fields (password hash, email, internal IDs, roles, tokens), it crosses a trust boundary into the rendering layer and may be accidentally rendered or leaked via template errors/debug.

Impact: Accidental exposure of sensitive user attributes in rendered HTML or error pages; increased blast radius if templates are compromised (e.g., via XSS) because more sensitive data is available in-page.

Fix: Only pass a minimal, explicitly whitelisted dict of safe fields to templates (e.g., id, display_name). Ensure get_auth_user returns a sanitized view model, not a DB row/model with secrets.

Session fixation risk: session ID not regenerated on successful login

On successful login, the code sets session['user_id'] but does not regenerate/rotate the session identifier. If an attacker can set or predict a victim's session ID (e.g., via session fixation), the attacker can hijack the authenticated session.

Impact: Account/session hijacking after victim logs in using a fixed session ID controlled by attacker.

Fix: Regenerate the session identifier upon authentication (e.g., invalidate old session and create a new one). In aiohttp-session, use a new session or configured session renewal/rotation on login.

Login endpoint lacks rate limiting (brute force protection missing)

The login POST flow has no throttling, lockout, or per-IP/user rate limiting, enabling online password guessing attacks.

Impact: Credential stuffing/brute force leading to account compromise; increased load/DoS risk.

Fix: Add rate limiting (per IP and per username), exponential backoff, and/or temporary account lockouts; log and alert on repeated failures.

Insecure password hashing: MD5 used for password verification (weak hashing)

The login flow relies on `user.check_password(password)`. In this application context, password hashing is implemented with MD5 (weak, fast, and vulnerable to brute force/rainbow tables). Even though the hashing code is in another file, this is the authentication path where the weak hash is security-relevant.

Impact: If password hashes are leaked, attackers can crack passwords quickly and reuse them across accounts; increases risk of credential stuffing and account compromise.

Fix: Migrate to a strong adaptive password hashing algorithm (Argon2id, bcrypt, or scrypt) with per-user salts. Implement a rehash-on-login migration strategy.

CSRF protection likely missing/ineffective for state-changing POST endpoints (server-side not enforced)

The students POST handler performs state changes but does not validate CSRF tokens server-side. Templates include `_csrf_token` fields, but without server-side middleware/validation this provides no protection.

Impact: Attackers can force victims' browsers to submit POST requests (cross-site) to create/modify data, especially impactful when combined with missing auth/authorization.

Fix: Enable and enforce CSRF middleware globally and validate CSRF tokens on every state-changing request. Consider SameSite cookies as defense-in-depth.

Sensitive credential material exposed via DAO returning pwd_hash to callers (risk of leakage to templates/logs)

User.get() and User.get_by_username() select and return `pwd_hash` as part of the User object. Returning password hashes broadly increases the chance they are accidentally logged, serialized, or passed into templates/contexts, crossing trust boundaries and enabling offline cracking if exposed.

Impact: Password hash disclosure through secondary bugs (logging, template rendering, debug pages) enabling offline cracking and account takeover.

Fix: Avoid selecting/returning `pwd_hash` except where strictly needed for authentication; use a separate minimal auth query/object; ensure hashes are never rendered or logged.

Insecure session cookie attribute: Secure flag not set (cookie may be sent over HTTP)

Session cookie configuration does not set the Secure attribute. Unless enforced elsewhere, cookies may be transmitted over plaintext HTTP, exposing sessions to network interception.

Impact: Session cookie leakage on unencrypted connections (e.g., shared Wi-Fi, reverse proxy misconfig), enabling session hijacking.

Fix: Configure session cookies with secure=True when behind HTTPS. Ensure deployment terminates TLS and forwards appropriate headers; enforce HTTPS-only.

No rate limiting on state-changing POST endpoints (form submissions)

Multiple POST endpoints are exposed with no evidence of throttling/rate limiting. This enables brute-force/spam/DoS via repeated submissions (students/courses creation, evaluations, reviews, logout).

Impact: Resource exhaustion, database bloat, spam content injection, and service degradation.

Fix: Implement per-IP and per-user rate limiting for all form submission endpoints; add CAPTCHA or additional abuse controls for public endpoints.

Redis connection lacks authentication/TLS parameters (config schema omits password) enabling unauthorized access if network-exposed

Redis configuration schema includes host/port/db but no password/TLS options. If Redis is reachable beyond localhost/container network, attackers could connect without auth and read/modify session data. (CWE mapping forced by checklist; conceptually this is missing authentication/config hardening.)

Impact: Session tampering or theft by direct Redis access; potential privilege escalation by modifying session contents.

Fix: Add Redis auth/TLS fields to config schema and enforce them in connection setup (requirepass/ACL, TLS). Bind Redis to private network only and restrict firewall rules.

Potential XSS: evaluation errors rendered without escaping

Validation errors are rendered directly ({{ fieldname }} and {{ errors }}) without explicit escaping. If error content includes user input or crafted field names, this can become reflected XSS when autoescape is disabled.

Impact: Reflected XSS on evaluation error page; could target admins if they trigger validation errors.

Fix: Escape fieldname and errors (|e) and avoid embedding raw user input in error messages. Enable autoescape.

CSRF token generation exists but may be ineffective without middleware enforcement

A CSRF token is generated and stored in session for templates, but since CSRF middleware is disabled, tokens may be present in forms without being validated. This creates a false sense of protection and increases risk of missing CSRF coverage.

Impact: Developers may assume CSRF is enforced when it is not; endpoints remain CSRF-vulnerable.

Fix: Enable CSRF middleware and ensure all state-changing requests validate tokens. Add tests to ensure CSRF failures are enforced.

Review creation endpoint lacks rate limiting (unthrottled form submission)

No throttling on review submissions, enabling spam and resource exhaustion.

Impact: Spam/DoS; database growth.

Fix: Add per-IP/user rate limiting and anti-spam controls (CAPTCHA, moderation, content limits).

Sensitive data in template context: full auth_user object passed to templates

The index handler passes the full auth_user model object into the template context. Model objects often contain sensitive fields (password hashes, internal IDs, admin flags, tokens). Even if templates currently render only names, this crosses a trust boundary and increases risk of accidental disclosure via template changes or debug output.

Impact: Potential exposure of sensitive user attributes in rendered HTML if templates are modified or if autoescape is disabled elsewhere; increases blast radius of template injection/XSS.

Fix: Pass only the minimal safe fields needed by templates (e.g., a dict with first_name/last_name/is_admin). Avoid passing full ORM/DAO objects to templates.

Input validation missing for student creation (unbounded/unsafe name)

Student name is taken directly from POST data with no server-side validation (length/charset) before storage. This can enable stored XSS (given autoescape is disabled) and resource abuse via very large payloads.

Impact: Stored XSS across pages that render student names; potential memory/DB bloat with oversized inputs.

Fix: Validate name (type, length limits, allowed characters) and escape on output; enforce request size limits.

Potential XSS/injection in error templates via unescaped error.status

error.status is rendered into HTML and a URL without explicit escaping. Typically status is numeric and safe, but if error object is attacker-influenced or misused, this could become an injection sink when autoescape is disabled.

Impact: Low likelihood XSS/HTML injection; could become exploitable if error.status is not strictly an int.

Fix: Ensure error.status is an int and enable autoescape. Consider using |e even for numeric fields for defense-in-depth.

Potential XSS/injection in 50x error template via unescaped error.status

Same pattern as 40x: error.status is rendered without explicit escaping. Usually safe but risky if error object is attacker-controlled in any way and autoescape is disabled.

Impact: Low likelihood injection; defense-in-depth issue.

Fix: Enable autoescape and ensure error.status is numeric.

Potential XSS: last_visited rendered without escaping on index page

last_visited is rendered without explicit escaping. It is sourced from the session and initially set server-side, but if an attacker can tamper with session storage or if other code writes user-controlled values into this key, it becomes an XSS sink when autoescape is disabled.

Impact: Low likelihood reflected/stored XSS depending on session integrity.

Fix: Enable autoescape and/or escape last_visited with |e. Keep session data integrity protected.
